title: Map file formats
text: The library persists two kinds of mapping file. Both are NetCDF and both make the (expensive) weight computation a one-time cost.

title: Map file formats
text:
Native map file (map_class)
Written by map_write / read by map_read (coordinates_mapping.f90).

Filename: <fldr>/map_<name1>_<name2>_<nmax>.nc (e.g. maps/map_CCSM3-T42_GRL-20KM_10.nc).
Keyed by source name, target name, and max_neighbors, so a change in any of them produces a distinct cache file.


Layout
The per-target neighbor structure (map(:)%…) is flattened into packed “neighbor vector” arrays so the ragged neighbor counts fit a NetCDF file.



Dimension
Size
Meaning




point
npts
target points


neighbor_vec
Σ neighbors
packed neighbor links


point_4
npts·4
bilinear corner indices


planetpar
3
planet a, f, R


projpar
5
projection lambda, phi, alpha, x_e, y_n


xc/yc or lon/lat
grid axes
present when is_grid










Variable
Meaning




mp_vec_nn(point)
neighbor count per target point


mp_vec_i(neighbor_vec)
source index of each link


mp_vec_quadrant, mp_vec_border
quadrant / border flag per link


mp_vec_x, mp_vec_y, mp_vec_dist
source coords and distance per link


mp_vec_weight
Shepard inverse-distance weight per link


mp_vec_area
conservative overlap-area weight per link


mp_vec_iquad(point_4)
the 4 bilinear corner indices


mp_vec_alpha1/2/3(point)
bilinear coefficients


x, y, lon, lat
target coordinates (2D when is_grid)


metadata
mtype, units, is_cartesian, is_projection, is_lon180, is_same_map, is_grid, npts, nmax, xy_conv, plus planet_info, proj_info



On read, unpack_neighbors rebuilds the per-point pt_wts_class array from the packed vectors.






Note



Because both the neighbor distances and the areas are stored, a single cached map can drive any map_field method (nn / bilinear / quadrant / shepard) without recomputation.

title: Map file formats
text:
SCRIP map file (map_scrip_class)
Produced by cdo and read by map_scrip_load (coordinates_mapping_scrip.f90). This is the standard SCRIP remapping format, a sparse weight matrix as parallel link arrays.







Component
Meaning




src_grid_size, dst_grid_size
source / target cell counts


num_links
number of source→target links


num_wgts
weights per link (1 for first-order conservative / bilinear)


src_address(num_links)
source cell index of each link


dst_address(num_links)
destination cell index of each link


remap_matrix(num_wgts, num_links)
the weight(s)


grid geometry
center & corner lon/lat, masks, areas, fractions



Application gathers all links with a given dst_address and forms the weighted sum Σ wⱼ var1(src_addressⱼ) / Σ wⱼ.

title: Map file formats
text:
Relationship
The two formats encode the same idea — a sparse source→target weight matrix — in different shapes. The native format is neighbor-centric (good for IDW and bilinear, where per-link distance/quadrant matter); the SCRIP format is link-centric (good for conservative weights). The improvement proposal suggests converging on a single internal sparse-weight representation that both the native and conservative paths populate, so map_field applies them uniformly.

title: Improvement proposal
text: This is a focused design document for modernising the two operations that dominate cost — the neighbor search and the conservative weight calculation — plus a set of structural cleanups. The non-negotiable constraint is that the user-facing API stays put: map_init and map_field keep their current signatures and semantics. Everything proposed here is an internal replacement that those two routines call into.

title: Improvement proposal
text:
Goals & constraints







Goal
Constraint




Neighbor search in seconds, not minutes, on large grids
map_init(map, src, dst, max_neighbors, …) signature unchanged


Conservative weights computed analytically in-house
map_field(map, name, v1, v2, method=…) signature unchanged


Drop the runtime cdo / YAC dependency for conservative remapping
existing cached map files should still load (or migrate cleanly)


Lon-lat conservative remapping supported (currently planar-only)
results match CDO/YAC to round-off on test cases



The yardstick is YAC: it computes mapping weights in seconds where the current brute-force + subgrid approach takes minutes. The two ideas that get us there are a spatial index (KD-tree) for the search and analytic polygon clipping for conservative overlap areas — exactly the building blocks YAC uses.

title: Improvement proposal
text:
1. KD-tree neighbor search

Problem
map_calc_weights (coordinates_mapping.f90:450) is:
for each target point i in 1..M:          ! M target points
   for each source point j in 1..N:       ! N source points  -> O(N·M)
       if |lat_j - lat_i| < lat_lim:       ! weak pre-filter
           d = distance(i, j)
           insert-sorted into neighbor list ! cshift = O(nmax) per insert
Complexity is O(N·M) distance evaluations (the latitude band only shaves a constant factor), and each accepted neighbor triggers an O(nmax) array shift. For two ~10⁶-cell grids this is the minutes-long step. It is OpenMP-parallel over targets, which helps but does not change the asymptotics.


Design
Build one KD-tree over the source points, then query k nearest neighbors per target point.

Build: O(N log N), once per map_init.
Query: O(log N + k) per target → O(M·(log N + k)) total. For N = M = 10⁶ this is ~10⁴× fewer distance evaluations than the double loop.
Parallel: queries are independent → OpenMP over targets, as today.


Handling the sphere
The subtlety is that “nearest” must respect geodesic distance and wrap across the ±180° seam and the poles. The clean, standard trick is to embed lon/lat on the unit sphere in 3D and build the KD-tree in (x,y,z):
xyz = [ cos(lat)*cos(lon), cos(lat)*sin(lon), sin(lat) ]
Chord distance in 3D is monotonic in great-circle distance, so the k nearest in 3D Euclidean space are exactly the k nearest geodesically. This removes all seam/pole special-casing for free, and it unifies the three current cases — lon-lat, same-projection Cartesian, cross-projection — into one tree:







Case
Tree coordinates




same Cartesian / same projection
the projected x, y (optionally z=0)


lon-lat or cross-projection
the 3D unit-sphere embedding above



After the k candidates are found, the exact geodesic distance (or Cartesian distance for the projected case) is computed once per candidate using the existing planet_distance / cartesian_distance, preserving today’s numbers in pt_wts_class%dist. The KD-tree only changes which candidates are evaluated, not the distance metric stored.


Proposed module
A new self-contained module, no external deps:
module kdtree
  type kdtree_t
     ! flat arrays: split dims, split values, point indices, child pointers
  end type

  ! Build from N points (2D projected or 3D sphere-embedded)
  subroutine kdtree_build(tree, pts_xyz)          ! pts_xyz(ndim, N)

  ! k nearest neighbors to a query point; returns indices + squared dists
  subroutine kdtree_knn(tree, q_xyz, k, idx, d2, nfound)

  ! all neighbors within a radius (for radius/shepard and conservative search)
  subroutine kdtree_radius(tree, q_xyz, r, idx, d2, nfound)
end module
A left-balanced array-backed KD-tree (median split on the widest dimension) keeps it allocation-light and cache-friendly; ~300 lines of Fortran, no recursion needed at query time if implemented with an explicit stack.


Where it slots in
map_calc_weights is rewritten as:
build tree over source xyz                    ! O(N log N)
!$omp parallel do
for each target i:
    kdtree_knn(tree, q_i, max_neighbors, idx, d2, n)
    for each candidate: compute exact dist, quadrant, border, weight
    store into map%map(i)                       ! no cshift; already k-sized
The sorted-insertion cshift logic disappears (the KNN result is already the k nearest; sort k items once). Quadrant assignment, border detection, and weight = 1/dist^p are unchanged. map_init, the cache format, and map_field are untouched.
The same kdtree_radius query also replaces the bounding-box which(...) linear scan used by the conservative neighbor search (below) and by interp_nearest_fast.



Effort & risk







Effort
Medium — one new module (~300 LOC) + rewrite of map_calc_weights inner loop


Risk
Low — drop-in; outputs validated against the existing brute-force result on test grids (bit-comparable distances, identical neighbor sets)


Payoff
The headline minutes→seconds win for nn/bilinear/quadrant/shepard

title: Improvement proposal
text:
2. Analytic conservative weights (spherical polygon clipping)

Problem
Conservative overlap areas are estimated by subgrid point sampling (interp2D_conservative.f90:504, coordinates_mapping.f90:758): each source cell is diced into nx×ny points (up to ~31²), each tested with a ray-cast point-in-polygon, and the overlap is the fraction inside × cell area. This is:

approximate — the overlap area is quantised to 1/(nx·ny);
slow — cost grows ~quadratically with the source/target resolution ratio;
planar only — the embedded copy explicitly does not support lon-lat (coordinates_mapping.f90:782).



Design
Compute each overlap area exactly by clipping the source cell polygon against the target cell polygon and integrating the area of the clipped result — the YAC approach.

Step 1 — find candidate source cells
Use kdtree_radius (from §1) around each target cell center with radius ≈ the target cell diagonal + max source cell diagonal. This replaces the linear bounding-box scan and bounds the candidate set to O(1) cells per target.


Step 2 — clip polygons
For each candidate source cell, clip it against the (convex, rectangular) target cell with Sutherland–Hodgman:
clip source-polygon S against each edge E of target-polygon T:
    keep the portion of S on T's interior side of E
result R = clipped polygon (empty if no overlap)
overlap_area = area(R)
weight = overlap_area / area(T)        ! for flux-conserving "mean"
Sutherland–Hodgman is exact, O(|S|·|T|) per pair (here 4·4), and needs only line-segment intersection + inside-edge test — no point-in-polygon sampling, no ray casting. For axis-aligned rectangular cells it simplifies to interval overlaps, but the general clip keeps it correct for rotated/projected cells.


Step 3 — planar vs spherical








Mode
Clip in…
Area via…




Cartesian / same projection
projected x, y plane
shoelace (cartesian_area)


lon-lat (large cells)
on the sphere: edges are great-circle arcs; clip with spherical half-space tests
planet_area (geodesic, already in planet.f90)



The planar clip covers the common projected-grid case exactly. The spherical clip — replacing each Cartesian edge test with a great-circle/point-side test and integrating with the existing geodesic planet_area — is what unlocks correct lon-lat conservative remapping, which the current code lacks. This is precisely YAC’s algorithm and is the one genuinely new piece of geometry.






TipIncremental path



Ship the planar Sutherland–Hodgman clip first (covers projected target grids, the dominant use case, exactly and fast). Add the spherical clip as a second stage to retire the lon-lat gap and fully match YAC. Each stage is independently testable against CDO output.




Where it slots in
map_conservative_init keeps its signature; internally it (a) queries the KD-tree for candidates and (b) calls a new cell_overlap_area(src_cell, dst_cell, latlon) instead of interpconserv1_weights. The result fills the same pt_wts_class%area array, so map_field_conservative_map / map_field_conservative_map1 apply it unchanged. map_field(method="con") can then route to this path with no external cdo call.



Effort & risk







Effort
Medium (planar) + Medium (spherical) — one polygon_clip module reusing polygons/planet


Risk
Medium — geometry edge cases (degenerate/zero-area clips, antimeridian, poles). Mitigated by validating area conservation (Σ weights·areas) and diffing against CDO gencon on the existing test grids


Payoff
Removes the runtime cdo/YAC dependency; exact (not sampled) weights; lon-lat support; orders-of-magnitude faster than subgrid sampling at high resolution ratios

title: Improvement proposal
section: 3. Unify the weight store and apply path
text:
3. Unify the weight store and apply path
Today there are three conservative implementations and two separate pt_wts_class / calc_grid_total definitions (interp2D_conservative.f90, coordinates_mapping_conservative.f90, coordinates_mapping.f90), plus a separate SCRIP remap_matrix representation. They all encode the same object: a sparse source→target weight matrix.
Proposal: a single internal sparse-weight type, populated by every path (IDW, bilinear, conservative, or imported SCRIP), and one apply routine.
type weight_map_t
    integer :: n_src, n_dst, n_links
    integer,  allocatable :: src(:), dst(:)   ! link addresses (SCRIP-style)
    real(dp), allocatable :: w(:)             ! link weights
end type

! one applicator for every method
subroutine weight_map_apply(wm, var1, var2, op, missing_value)
    ! op = "mean" | "sum" | "count" | "stdev"
Benefits:

map_field and map_scrip_field share one tested kernel; method="mean", "count", "stdev" live in one place instead of three.
The native and SCRIP map files become two serialisations of the same object — one loader, one writer (see Map file formats).
Conservative area, Shepard weight, and bilinear alpha all reduce to link weights, so the per-method branching in map_field shrinks.

Keep pt_wts_class as the neighbor-centric view (it carries quadrant/border needed during search) but derive the link arrays from it for application.







Effort
Low–Medium (mechanical consolidation + tests)




Risk
Low — pure refactor behind unchanged public API


Payoff
Removes duplication, one place to optimise/parallelise, simpler cache code

title: Improvement proposal
text:
4. Smaller generalisations & cleanups








Item
Current
Proposed




index utilities
unique_*, maxcount are O(n²)
hash/sort-based O(n log n); these run inside conservative count/stdev


Projection coverage
oblique/polar stereographic + LAEA only
add Lambert conformal, Mercator, rotated-pole behind the same mtype/proj dispatch (the OBLIMAP dispatch table makes this additive)


Planets
hard-coded WGS84 + Spherical Earth
data-driven planet_init (name → params table) so other bodies/ellipsoids are one-line additions


grid_to_cdo wraplon bug
wraplon=.TRUE. mishandles the lon=0 seam
obsolete once §2 removes the CDO path; until then, fix or guard it


Single/double precision
pt_wts_class mixes sp/dp; prec=sp globally
make the working precision a single compile-time switch; store weights in dp to avoid conservative-sum drift


Bilinear as weights
special-cased alpha1/2/3 + calc_bilin_point
express as 4 link weights in the unified store (§3); one apply path


Repeated grid→points conversions
each map_init overload re-converts
cache the points view on the grid object

title: Improvement proposal
text:
Suggested sequencing

KD-tree module + rewire map_calc_weights (§1). Self-contained, immediate win for the methods you use most, no format change. Validate against current output.
Unified weight store (§3). Refactor with the search already faster; simplifies the next step.
Planar analytic conservative clip (§2, stage 1). Removes cdo for projected target grids.
Spherical conservative clip (§2, stage 2). Retires the lon-lat gap; full CDO/YAC parity.
Cleanups (§4) opportunistically alongside the above.

Throughout, map_init and map_field remain the only entry points your downstream code calls — the speed-up and the removal of the cdo dependency are entirely under the hood.






NoteValidation strategy



Each step is gated on reproducing existing results: the KD-tree must return the same neighbor sets as the brute-force loop; the analytic conservative weights must (a) conserve area to round-off and (b) match cdo gencon on the repository test grids (test_ccsm3.f90, test_climber.f90, the MAR/ETOPO tests). Only once parity is shown does the cdo path get retired.

title: Conservative remapping
text:
Conservative remapping preserves the area-integral of a field when moving between grids — essential for fluxes, mass, and any quantity that must be globally conserved. The library implements it in-house by subgrid point sampling, and also offers an external CDO/SCRIP path that is preferred in practice for large grids.
Modules:

title: Conservative remapping
text:
Public API








Routine
Module
Purpose




map_conservative_init(map, grid1, grid2)
interp2D_conservative
build overlap-area weights grid1→grid2


map_field_conservative_map(map, varname, var1, var2, fill, missing_value, mask_pack)
interp2D_conservative
apply weights (area-weighted mean)


map_field_conservative_grid(grid1, grid2, varname, var1, var2, …)
interp2D_conservative
one-shot init+apply between two grids


map_field_conservative_smooth(map, map_hilo, grid1, grid2, …)
interp2D_conservative
conservative + smoothing variant


interpconserv1_weights(x, y, dx, dy, xout, yout, dxout, dyout, latlon)
interp2D_conservative
overlap area of source cells with one target cell


map_field_conservative_map1(mp, varname, var1, var2, method, …)
…_conservative
apply weights with method = mean/count/stdev


calc_grid_total(x, y, var, xlim, ylim)
both
area-integral of a field over a region

title: Conservative remapping
section: How the weights are computed (subgrid sampling)
text:
How the weights are computed (subgrid sampling)
For each target cell, the overlap area contributed by each nearby source cell is estimated by dicing the source cell into a regular grid of sample points and counting how many fall inside the target cell polygon (interpconserv1_weights, interp2D_conservative.f90:504):
! Target cell as a rectangular polygon
pol = create_polygon([xout±dxout/2], [yout±dyout/2])

! For each candidate source cell, sample nx×ny subgrid points
nx = max(1, int(dx/dxout))      ! (the copy in coordinates_mapping.f90 uses max(5, …))
ny = max(1, int(dy/dyout))
do j = 1, ny
  do i = 1, nx
     x1 = (x-dx/2) + dx*(i-0.5)/nx
     y1 = (y-dy/2) + dy*(j-0.5)/ny
     if (point_in_polygon(x1, y1, pol)) npts_in = npts_in + 1
  end do
end do

! Overlap area  ≈  (fraction of points inside) × source cell area
area(now) = dble(npts_in)/dble(nx*ny) * dx*dy
The candidate source cells are found with an axis-aligned bounding-box scan (which(...), a linear pass over all source points) sized by the coarser of the two cell spacings.

Applying the weights
! area-weighted mean, skipping missing source values
var2(i) = sum(var1(ii)*area, mask=area>0) / sum(area, mask=area>0)
map_field_conservative_map1 additionally offers method="count" (modal / most-frequent value, weighted by area) and method="stdev" (area-weighted standard deviation).

title: Conservative remapping
text:
Cost and limitations







Property
Status




Accuracy
approximate — error ∝ 1/(subgrid points); a step function of the resolution ratio


Cost per target cell
O(n_neighbors × nx × ny × cost(point-in-polygon))


Resolution-ratio scaling
nx,ny ∝ dx/dxout, so cost grows ~quadratically with the ratio (up to ~31×31 sub-points)


Lon-lat support
not supported in the embedded copy (coordinates_mapping.f90:782); assumes planar rectangular cells


Neighbor search
bounding-box linear scan, no spatial index



This is why, for production conservative remapping on large grids, the library shells out to cdo (which uses YAC’s analytic spherical clipping). The improvement proposal replaces the subgrid sampling with analytic polygon clipping so the in-house path matches CDO’s accuracy and speed, and drops the external dependency.

title: SCRIP / CDO remapping path
text:
For heavy remapping — especially conservative remapping on large grids — the library outsources weight generation to the external cdo command-line tool, which uses YAC internally to compute SCRIP-format weights in seconds. The library writes grid descriptions, invokes cdo, reads the resulting weight file, and applies it.
Modules:

title: SCRIP / CDO remapping path
text:
The SCRIP weight format
A SCRIP map is a NetCDF file storing a sparse remapping matrix as parallel link arrays. The map_scrip_class holds:

src_grid_size, dst_grid_size, grid dims, num_links, num_wgts.
Grid geometry: center lon/lat, corner lon/lat, masks, areas, fractions.
The sparse matrix as three arrays:

src_address(num_links) — source cell index of each link,
dst_address(num_links) — destination cell index of each link,
remap_matrix(num_wgts, num_links) — the weight(s) of each link (num_wgts = 1 for first-order conservative / nearest / bilinear).


Applying the map is then: for each destination cell k, gather the links with dst_address == k and form var2(k) = Σ wⱼ·var1(src_addressⱼ) / Σ wⱼ.

title: SCRIP / CDO remapping path
text:
User API

map_scrip_init
subroutine map_scrip_init(mps, grid1, grid2, method, fldr, load, clean)








Argument
Default
Meaning




mps (inout)
–
SCRIP map object to fill


grid1, grid2
–
source and target grids


method
"con"
"con" (conservative), "bil" (bilinear), "bic" (bicubic)


fldr
"maps"
directory for grid-desc + map files


load
.TRUE.
load an existing map file if present


clean
–
delete the temporary grid/desc files afterwards



It writes CDO grid descriptions for both grids, writes the source grid to NetCDF, runs cdo, then loads the resulting SCRIP file via map_scrip_load.


map_scrip_field
subroutine map_scrip_field(map, var_name, var1, var2, mask2, method, reset, &
                           missing_value, mask_pack, fill_method, &
                           filt_method, filt_par, verbose)







Argument
Meaning




var1, var2
source / target 2D fields


method
"mean" (conservative weighted mean), "count" (weighted mode), "stdev"


fill_method
post-fill of unmapped cells: "weighted", "nn", "none"


filt_method
optional post-smoothing: "gaussian", "gaussian-fast", "poisson"


filt_par
filter parameters (e.g. σ)


missing_value, mask_pack, reset, verbose
as in map_field



The design intentionally mirrors map_init / map_field so switching a workflow between the in-house and CDO paths is a near drop-in change.


Other entry points







Routine
Purpose




map_scrip_init_from_griddesc(map, src_name, dst_name, fldr, src_nc, method, load)
the active path that builds the cdo gen… command


map_scrip_load(map, src_name, dst_name, fldr, method)
read a pre-computed SCRIP NetCDF file

title: SCRIP / CDO remapping path
text:
The CDO commands
The weight generation (map_scrip_init_from_griddesc, coordinates_mapping_scrip.f90:853) builds and runs:
cdo gen<method>,<target_griddesc> -setgrid,<source_griddesc> <source.nc> <map.nc>
# e.g.
cdo gencon,grid_GRL.txt -setgrid,grid_CCSM3.txt ccsm3.nc map_CCSM3_GRL.nc
Grid descriptions are produced by grid_to_cdo:

grid_cdo_write_desc_short(grid, fldr) — writes a compact CDO grid description (grid_<name>.txt): gridtype (lonlat / projection / cartesian / generic), sizes, axis first/inc, and projection metadata (origin lon/lat, oblique angle, planet axes) as a grid_mapping.
grid_cdo_write_desc_explicit_proj(lon2D, lat2D, …) — explicit curvilinear description with per-cell corner bounds computed from neighbouring centers.
grid_cdo_write_desc_explicit_latlon(lon, lat, …, wraplon) — 1D lon/lat with optional periodic wrap.
call_system_cdo(cdo_cmd) — runs the command via execute_command_line, checks the exit status, and stops on error.

title: SCRIP / CDO remapping path
text:
Caveats

Requires cdo to be installed and on PATH at runtime — an external, out-of-process dependency.
The explicit curvilinear path has had trouble with CDO rejecting missing cell corners (“Target grid cell corner coordinates missing!”), so the compact …_short descriptions are preferred.
grid_cdo_write_desc_explicit_latlon’s wraplon=.TRUE. is documented as buggy (mis-handles the lon=0 seam).

Removing this external dependency — by computing SCRIP-equivalent conservative weights in-house with analytic clipping — is the central goal of the improvement proposal.

title: coordinates
text:
The coordinates library manages grids and arbitrary sets of points, and maps fields between them. It handles projection between coordinate systems, nearest-neighbor and conservative interpolation, and caches the (expensive) mapping calculation to disk so it is only ever done once per grid pair.
It is the workhorse behind regional/global climate and ice-sheet model pre-processing: taking observations or model output on one grid (a global lat-lon grid, an unstructured set of station locations, polygon data) and remapping them onto another (e.g. a regional oblique-stereographic grid over Greenland or Antarctica).

title: coordinates
text:
The mental model
Everything is built on points. A grid is just a structured 2D arrangement of points, and a map records, for each target point, which source points feed it and with what weight. Because the design pushes all the state into these three objects, mapping a field between two domains takes only three steps and no temporary bookkeeping variables.





flowchart LR
    A["grid_class / points_class<br/>(source)"] -->|map_init| M["map_class<br/>(neighbors + weights,<br/>cached to NetCDF)"]
    B["grid_class / points_class<br/>(target)"] -->|map_init| M
    M -->|map_field| F["field on target grid"]
    V["field on source grid"] --> F

title: coordinates
text:
The three-step example
use coord

type(grid_class) :: gCCSM3, gGRL
type(map_class)  :: map1

! 1. Define each grid (global lat-lon source, regional stereographic target)
call grid_init(gCCSM3, name="CCSM3-T42", mtype="latlon", units="degrees", &
               x0=0.d0,   dx=2.d0, nx=180, y0=-90.d0, dy=2.d0, ny=90)

call grid_init(gGRL, name="GRL-20KM", mtype="stereographic", units="kilometers", &
               dx=20.d0, nx=76, dy=20.d0, ny=151, &
               lambda=-40.d0, phi=72.d0, alpha=7.5d0)

! 2. Build (or load from cache) the mapping between them
call map_init(map1, gCCSM3, gGRL, max_neighbors=10)

! 3. Map a field
call map_field(map1, "t2m", var_ccsm3, var_grl, mask_map, method="quadrant")
The first map_init for a given grid pair computes neighbors and writes a NetCDF map file under maps/. Every later run loads that file instead of recomputing — so the up-front cost (often >1 minute on large grids) is paid once.

title: coordinates
text:
What’s documented here

Concepts — the points / grid / map data model and the philosophy of points as the primitive.
API — the public interface, organised by module: coordinates (building points & grids), mapping (map_init / map_field), conservative remapping, the SCRIP/CDO path, and helper modules.
Reference — supported projections & planets and the on-disk map file formats.
Improvements — a focused design proposal for replacing the brute-force neighbor search with a KD-tree and the subgrid-sampling conservative weights with analytic spherical-polygon clipping, removing the external CDO dependency while keeping map_init / map_field unchanged.

title: coordinates
text:
Installation in brief
The library depends only on a Fortran compiler and NetCDF (the bundled NCIO wraps it). The optional conservative-remapping-via-CDO path additionally calls the cdo command-line tool at runtime. Build the static library with make coordinates and link against libcoordinates/include/libcoordinates.a, then use coord in your program.






Note



This documentation was reconstructed by reading the source. Line references (e.g. coordinates_mapping.f90:450) point at the version of the code in this repository and are meant as navigation aids.

title: Concepts & data model
Concepts & data model
Concepts & data model
text:
The three objects








Object
Holds
Built by




points_class
1D vectors of locations (+ optional area, dx/dy)
points_init


grid_class
same, as 2D arrays, plus a grid_axis_class
grid_init


map_class
per-target-point neighbor list + weights
map_init



Convert freely between the first two with grid_to_points(grid, pts) and points_to_grid(pts, grid).

Coordinate-system metadata
Both points_class and grid_class carry the same descriptive fields:

mtype — the coordinate/map type string: latlon / latitude_longitude / gaussian, cartesian, or a projection (stereographic, polar_stereographic, lambert_azimuthal_equal_area).
units — axis units (degrees, kilometers, meters, …). A scalar xy_conv factor converts the stored axis units to meters for distance work.
planet — a planet_class (either "WGS84" or "Spherical Earth").
proj — a projection_class with parameters lambda, phi, alpha, x_e, y_n (only populated when is_projection).
is_cartesian, is_projection, is_lon180 — booleans that drive how distances and neighbors are computed.

See Projections & planets for the full set of supported values.

title: Concepts & data model
text:
How a field is mapped

map_init(map, src, dst, max_neighbors=…) finds, for every target point, its nearest source points (up to max_neighbors), records their indices, distances, quadrants, and inverse-distance weights, and (for structured grids) precomputes bilinear coefficients. The whole structure is cached to a NetCDF map file keyed by the two grid names and max_neighbors.
map_field(map, name, var_src, var_dst, …, method=…) applies those stored neighbors/weights to an actual field. The method (nn, bilinear, quadrant, radius/shepard) selects how the per-point neighbor values are combined.

Because the neighbor distances are stored, the same cached map supports any distance-based weighting scheme without recomputation — you can change method at map_field time for free.

title: Concepts & data model
text:
Distances
Distance is computed two ways, chosen automatically:

Cartesian / same projected system → Euclidean distance in the working units (cartesian_distance).
Otherwise (lon-lat or cross-projection) → geodesic distance on the planet ellipsoid via Karney’s GeographicLib algorithms, bundled as geodesic.f90 (planet_distance).

This is the rule of thumb stated throughout the codebase: unless both point sets live on the same Cartesian/projected system, distances are geodesic lon-lat calculations.

title: Concepts & data model
text:
Where the cost is
Two operations dominate runtime, and both are the focus of the improvement proposal:

Neighbor search in map_init is brute force — an O(N×M) double loop over source × target points with a latitude pre-filter, no spatial index.
Conservative weights are approximated by dicing each source cell into subgrid points and counting how many fall inside the target cell, rather than computing the polygon overlap analytically.

Both are correct but slow, which is why heavy conservative remapping is currently outsourced to the external cdo tool (which uses YAC internally).

title: Coordinates — points & grids
text: Module: coordinates (src/coordinates.f90). Defines the two geometry types and their constructors, conversions, I/O, and cell-area calculation.

title: Coordinates — points & grids
text:
Types

points_class
type points_class
    character(len=128) :: name        ! dataset name
    character(len=128) :: mtype       ! latlon | cartesian | stereographic | ...
    character(len=128) :: units       ! degrees | kilometers | meters | ...
    type(planet_class) :: planet      ! WGS84 or Spherical Earth

    logical :: is_cartesian, is_projection
    logical :: is_lon180              ! .TRUE. => lon in [-180,180]; .FALSE. => [0,360]
    type(projection_class) :: proj

    integer :: npts
    real(dp), allocatable :: x(:), y(:), lon(:), lat(:), area(:)
    real(dp), allocatable :: dx(:), dy(:)   ! per-point cell spacing (for conservative)
    integer,  allocatable :: border(:)
    real(dp) :: xy_conv               ! axis-units -> meters conversion factor
end type


grid_class
Same descriptive fields as points_class, but the location arrays are 2D and a structured-axis descriptor G is attached:
type grid_class
    ! ... same metadata fields as points_class ...
    integer :: npts
    real(dp), allocatable :: x(:,:), y(:,:), lon(:,:), lat(:,:), area(:,:)
    integer,  allocatable :: border(:,:)
    real(dp) :: xy_conv
    type(grid_axis_class) :: G
end type

type grid_axis_class
    integer  :: nx, ny
    real(dp) :: x0, dx, y0, dy
    real(dp), allocatable :: x(:), y(:)
end type

title: Coordinates — points & grids
text:
Building a grid — grid_init
grid_init is a generic interface with four constructors. The most common is from options:
subroutine grid_init_from_opts(grid, name, mtype, units, &
                               planet, lon180, &
                               x, y, x0, dx, nx, y0, dy, ny, &
                               lambda, phi, alpha, x_e, y_n)








Argument
Required
Meaning




grid
✔ (out)
the grid object to fill


name
✔
grid name (used in cached map filenames)


mtype
✔
coordinate/map type string


units
✔
axis units


planet
–
"WGS84" (default) or "Spherical Earth"


lon180
–
longitude convention


x, y
–
explicit axis vectors (alternative to x0/dx/nx)


x0, dx, nx
–
x-axis origin / spacing / count


y0, dy, ny
–
y-axis origin / spacing / count


lambda, phi, alpha
–
projection centre lon, centre lat, oblique angle


x_e, y_n
–
false easting / northing



Other constructors in the interface:



Constructor
Builds a grid from…




grid_init_from_opts
explicit options (above)


grid_init_from_par
a parameter file (filename)


grid_init_from_grid
another grid (copy + override axes)


grid_init_from_points
an existing points_class




Examples
! Global 2° lat-lon grid
call grid_init(g, name="CCSM3-T42", mtype="latlon", units="degrees", &
               x0=0.d0, dx=2.d0, nx=180, y0=-90.d0, dy=2.d0, ny=90)

! Regional oblique-stereographic grid over Greenland
call grid_init(g, name="GRL-20KM", mtype="stereographic", units="kilometers", &
               dx=20.d0, nx=76, dy=20.d0, ny=151, &
               lambda=-40.d0, phi=72.d0, alpha=7.5d0)

title: Coordinates — points & grids
text:
Building a point set — points_init
Generic interface with five constructors. From options:
subroutine points_init_from_opts(pts, name, mtype, units, &
                                 planet, lon180, &
                                 x, y, dx, dy, &
                                 lambda, phi, alpha, x_e, y_n, &
                                 latlon, verbose)
x(:) and y(:) are the required location vectors. If latlon=.TRUE. the supplied x,y are interpreted as lon,lat. Per-point dx,dy may be supplied for conservative remapping. The other constructors mirror grid_init:



Constructor
Builds points from…




points_init_from_opts
explicit x,y vectors + options


points_init_from_par
a parameter file


points_init_from_file
a data file (with skip header lines)


points_init_from_points
another point set


points_init_from_grid
a grid

title: Coordinates — points & grids
text:
Converting between representations
subroutine grid_to_points(grid, pts, mask_pack, define)   ! 2D grid -> 1D points
subroutine points_to_grid(pts, grid, mask_pack, define)   ! 1D points -> 2D grid
mask_pack(:,:) (optional, logical) restricts the conversion to selected cells — this is how the library works with sparse subsets of a grid. define controls whether the target object’s metadata is (re)initialised.

title: Coordinates — points & grids
text:
Allocation & comparison helpers
call grid_allocate(grid, var)      ! allocate var to grid shape; generic over
                                   ! integer / float / double / logical
call points_allocate(pts, var)     ! same, for a point set

logical = compare_coord(pts1, pts2)   ! do two sets share a coordinate system?
logical = compare_coord(grid1, grid2)
grid_allocate / points_allocate are the idiomatic way to size a field array to match a grid or point set without restating dimensions.

title: Coordinates — points & grids
text:
Nearest point
idx = pts_which_nearest(pts, x, y, latlon)   ! index of nearest point to (x,y)

title: Coordinates — points & grids
text:
Cell areas
Cell area is computed when a grid is initialised (grid_area, coordinates.f90:141). For each cell the four corner vertices are constructed (extending by the cell spacing at boundaries, midpoints in the interior) and the area is integrated:

Cartesian grids → shoelace formula in the working units (cartesian_area).
Projected grids → each corner is inverse-projected to lon/lat, then the spherical/ellipsoidal polygon area is integrated (planet_area, using GeographicLib).
Lat-lon grids → planet_area directly on the lon/lat corners.

Areas are divided by xy_conv² so they end up in the grid’s own units. For a bare points_class (no grid structure) area defaults to 1.0 until set by a grid_to_points conversion.

title: Coordinates — points & grids
text:
I/O & printing
call grid_write(grid, filename, ...)     ! write grid coordinates to NetCDF
call points_write(pts, filename, ...)
call grid_print(grid)                     ! summary to stdout
call points_print(pts)

title: Helper modules
text: Beyond the core geometry and mapping, the library bundles a set of general-purpose modules for interpolation, filtering, polygon tests, and subsetting. They are all re-exported through the umbrella coord module.

title: Helper modules
text:
Interpolation

interp1D — 1D interpolation







Routine
Method




interp_linear(x, y, xout)
piecewise linear (scalar or vector xout), with edge extrapolation


interp_spline(x, y, xout)
natural cubic spline


interp_align(x, y, xout, missing_value, tol)
nearest-match alignment within tolerance





interp2D — 2D interpolation & gap-filling on structured grids







Routine
Purpose




interp_bilinear(x, y, z, xout, yout)
bilinear interpolation with missing-value handling


interp_nearest(x, y, z, xout, yout)
nearest-neighbor


interp_nearest_fast(x, y, z, xout, yout, max_dist_fac)
nearest-neighbor with a distance cutoff


fill_weighted / fill_nearest / fill_mean(z, missing_value)
fill missing cells from neighbors


fill_poisson(var2d, missing_value, method, wraplon)
fill by Poisson relaxation


filter_poisson(var2d, mask, tol, missing_value, wrapx)
Poisson smoothing over a mask


diffuse(z, iter, missing_value)
diffusive smoothing/fill


limit_gradient(z, dx, dy, grad_lim, iter_max)
clamp local gradients





interp_time — temporal interpolation







Routine
Purpose




time_average(var)
average along the time axis (2D/3D/4D interfaces)


convert_monthly_daily_*(mon, day, days)
spline-expand 12 monthly values to daily, cycling Dec↔︎Jan

title: Helper modules
text:
Filtering & smoothing

gaussian_filter







Routine
Purpose




filter_gaussian(var, sigma, dx, mask, truncate)
masked Gaussian smoothing (reference)


filter_gaussian_fast(var, sigma, dx, mask)
faster padded variant


gaussian_kernel(sigma, kernel, truncate)
build a Gaussian kernel


convolve(input, weights, output, mask)
masked convolution (reflection tiling)


diffuse2D(input, output, dx, mask)
five-point Laplacian diffusion


hgrad(u0, dx, norm)
horizontal gradient magnitude





loess







Routine
Purpose




loess_smooth(x, y, L, missing_value)
locally-weighted (LOWESS) 1D smoothing, robust to outliers; handles uneven spacing and missing values via internal regular-grid interpolation

title: Helper modules
text:
Geometry

polygons — point-in-polygon
Ray-casting point-in-polygon tests over a polygon type built from vertex arrays:







Routine
Purpose




create_polygon(pts) / create_polygon(xx, yy)
build a polygon


point_in_polygon(x, y, pol)
single-point test


points_1D_is_inside_poly / points_2D_is_inside_poly
vectorised tests


point_is_inside_points, points_*_is_inside_points
test against raw vertex arrays



These are used by the subgrid-sampling conservative remapping and for clipping polygon datasets to a domain.

title: Helper modules
text:
Subsetting & multigrid

subset / subset2 — sparse / downsampled grid representations
A subset_class couples a high-resolution grid, a packing mask, and the maps between a packed 1D point representation and the full 2D grid. This lets you work on a sparse or coarsened subset of a grid and map back losslessly.







Routine
Purpose




subset_init(sub, grid, npts, factor, …)
build a subset at a given refinement factor


subset_redefine(sub, …, mask_pack, …)
redefine the active points from a mask


subset_to_grid(sub, var1D, var2D, mask_pack)
unpack points → grid


subset_to_points(sub, var2D, var1D, mask_pack)
pack grid → points


subset_gen_mask(mask_pack, var, npts, min_spacing, method)
build a spacing-based mask



subset2 adds separate to/from-subset maps and a sigma option for weighted mapping.


grid_gen — multigrid hierarchies
multigrid_init(mgrid, grid, dx) builds a stack of progressively coarser grids (validating integer coarsening ratios) and the map_init maps between levels, for hierarchical/multigrid workflows.

title: Helper modules
text:
Vertical coordinate

coordinates_sigma
points_sigma_init(pts, name, units) defines a 0→1 sigma vertical coordinate (equidistant or exponentially deformed). Currently partial — points_sigma_update is a stub.

title: Helper modules
text:
Index utilities

index
Small array helpers used throughout: which(x, ind) (R-style boolean → index), unique_dble / unique_float (unique values within a tolerance), maxcount (weighted mode). Note: unique_* and maxcount are O(n²) and index provides no spatial-search structure — the neighbor search in coordinates_mapping is brute force. See Improvements.

title: Mapping — map_init & map_field
text: Module: coordinates_mapping (src/coordinates_mapping.f90). This is the core of the library: build a reusable mapping between two domains, then apply it to any number of fields.

title: Mapping — map_init & map_field
text:
Types

map_class
type map_class
    character(len=128) :: name1, name2     ! source, target names
    character(len=128) :: mtype, units
    type(planet_class) :: planet

    logical :: is_cartesian, is_projection, is_same_map, is_lon180
    type(projection_class) :: proj

    logical :: is_grid
    type(grid_axis_class) :: G             ! used when is_grid

    integer :: npts                         ! number of TARGET points
    real(dp), allocatable :: x(:), y(:), lon(:), lat(:)
    real(dp) :: xy_conv

    integer :: nmax                         ! max neighbors stored per target point
    integer, allocatable :: nn(:)
    type(pt_wts_class), allocatable :: map(:)   ! per-target neighbor structure
end type


pt_wts_class — the per-target neighbor record
type pt_wts_class
    integer :: n                                    ! number of neighbors
    integer,  allocatable :: i(:), quadrant(:), border(:)
    real(sp), allocatable :: x(:), y(:), dist(:), weight(:), area(:)
    ! bilinear only:
    integer,  allocatable :: iquad(:)               ! the 4 quadrant neighbors
    real(sp), allocatable :: alpha1(:), alpha2(:), alpha3(:)
end type

i — source-point indices.
dist — geodesic or Cartesian distance to each neighbor.
weight — Shepard inverse-distance weight, 1 / dist^p with p = 2.
area — conservative overlap-area weight (computed but not used by the standard map_field methods; see Conservative).
quadrant — which of the 4 quadrants (I–IV) the neighbor lies in relative to the target, used by the quadrant method and bilinear.
alpha1/2/3, iquad — precomputed bilinear coefficients and corner indices.

title: Mapping — map_init & map_field
text:
map_init
Generic interface with four overloads covering every source/target combination:
call map_init(map, grid1, grid2,  max_neighbors [, lat_lim, dist_max, fldr, load, save])
call map_init(map, grid1, pts2,   max_neighbors [, ...])
call map_init(map, pts1,  grid2,  max_neighbors [, ...])
call map_init(map, pts1,  pts2,   max_neighbors [, ...])



Argument
Default
Meaning




map (out)
–
mapping object to fill


source, target
–
any grid_class / points_class pair


max_neighbors
–
max source neighbors stored per target point


lat_lim
5.0°
latitude pre-filter half-window for the search


dist_max
large
hard distance cutoff for accepting a neighbor


fldr
"maps"
directory for the cached NetCDF map file


load
.TRUE.
load the cached map if it exists


save
.TRUE.
write the map to cache after computing



All four overloads convert their arguments to point sets and delegate to map_init_internal (coordinates_mapping.f90:185), which calls map_calc_weights to do the search.

Caching
The map is written to <fldr>/map_<name1>_<name2>_<nmax>.nc. On the next run with the same names and max_neighbors, map_init loads it instead of recomputing. Because neighbor distances are stored, the cached map supports any of the map_field methods without rebuilding. See Map file formats.






WarningThis is the slow step



map_calc_weights (coordinates_mapping.f90:450) is a brute-force O(n_source × n_target) double loop with a latitude pre-filter and per-insertion cshift of the neighbor arrays — no spatial index. On large grids the first build can take minutes. The cache hides this on subsequent runs, but the improvement proposal replaces it with a KD-tree.

title: Mapping — map_init & map_field
text:
map_field
Generic over element type (double / float / integer) and over grid/point source & target — 12 overloads, all delegating to map_field_points_points_double (coordinates_mapping.f90:1731):
subroutine map_field(map, name, var1, var2, mask2, method, &
                     radius, border, missing_value, mask_pack)







Argument
Meaning




map
the mapping from map_init


name
field name (for messages)


var1
source field (shape matches the source grid/points)


var2 (inout)
target field (shape matches target)


mask2 (out, opt)
which target points were successfully filled


method
interpolation method (table below)


radius (opt)
distance limit applied at apply-time


border (opt)
also fill target border points


missing_value (opt)
value treated as missing in var1 / written where unfilled


mask_pack (opt)
restrict interpolation to selected target points




Methods








method
Behaviour
Neighbors used




"nn" / "nearest"
nearest-neighbor value
1


"bilinear"
bilinear from the 4 quadrant neighbors (precomputed α)
4


"quadrant"
inverse-distance average of the nearest neighbor in each quadrant
≤4


"radius" / "shepard"
inverse-distance (Shepard, p=2) average of all neighbors within radius
≤ nmax



The weighting itself is var2 = Σ wᵢ·varᵢ / Σ wᵢ over the (optionally quadrant-restricted) neighbor set, skipping missing values (weighted_ave). Bilinear evaluates p₁ = z₂ + α₁(z₁−z₂), p₀ = z₃ + α₂(z₄−z₃), var = p₀ + α₃(p₁−p₀).


Example
call map_field(map1, "t2m", t2m_src, t2m_dst, mask_map, method="quadrant", &
               missing_value=-9999.d0)

title: Mapping — map_init & map_field
text:
Inspecting & comparing maps
call map_print(map)              ! npts, neighbor vector length, memory size
logical = compare_map(map1, grid2)   ! does an existing map target this grid?

title: Mapping — map_init & map_field
text:
Dependencies
coordinates_mapping builds on coordinates, planet (distances, quadrants, areas, weighted averages), oblimap_projection_module, polygons (for the conservative area weights), ncio (cache I/O), gaussian_filter, interp2D, and index.

title: Projections & planets
text: Module: oblimap_projection_module (src/projection_oblimap2.f90), ported from OBLIMAP2 (Reerink et al., 2010, Geosci. Model Dev.). Projections convert between geographic (lon, lat) and projected Cartesian (x, y) coordinates.

title: Projections & planets
text:
Supported mtype values








mtype string
Projected?
Notes




latitude_longitude / latlon / gaussian
no
geographic; x,y ≡ lon,lat


cartesian
no (generic)
plain planar coordinates, Euclidean distance


stereographic / polar_stereographic
yes
oblique & polar stereographic


lambert_azimuthal_equal_area
yes
oblique Lambert azimuthal equal-area



Projected types are realised in both spherical and ellipsoidal (Snyder) forms, selected automatically from the chosen planet:








Family
Sphere method
Ellipsoid method




Stereographic (oblique)
oblique_sg_projection
oblique_sg_projection_ellipsoid_snyder


Stereographic (polar special case)
—
polar_sg_projection_ellipsoid_snyder


Lambert azimuthal equal-area
oblique_laea_projection_snyder
oblique_laea_projection_ellipsoid_snyder

title: Projections & planets
text:
Projection parameters
Stored in projection_class; set via grid_init / points_init arguments of the same name. All angles in degrees.







Parameter
Meaning




lambda
longitude of the projection centre (central meridian)


phi
latitude of the projection centre (standard parallel)


alpha
oblique tangent angle — where the projection plane slices the sphere


x_e
false easting (x offset)


y_n
false northing (y offset)



alpha controls the plane’s intersection with the spherical surface: the smaller the domain, the smaller the alpha that minimises distortion. A helper optimal_alpha(R, nx, ny, dx, dy) estimates a good value for given grid dimensions.

Typical regional setups (Reerink et al., 2010)



Domain
lambda
phi
alpha




Greenland
320° (= −40°)
72°
7.5°


Antarctica
0°
−90°
19.0°



call grid_init(gGRL, name="GRL-20KM", mtype="stereographic", units="kilometers", &
               dx=20.d0, nx=76, dy=20.d0, ny=151, &
               lambda=-40.d0, phi=72.d0, alpha=7.5d0)

title: Projections & planets
text:
Forward & inverse transforms
Dispatched by the projection’s method:







Routine
Direction




oblimap_projection(lambda, phi, x_out, y_out, proj)
lon/lat → x/y


oblimap_projection_inverse(x, y, lambda_out, phi_out, proj)
x/y → lon/lat



Supporting routines include projection_init, same_projection (equivalence test, used by map_init to decide whether two domains share a system), optimal_alpha, and the rotation helpers rotation_projection / inverse_rotation_projection.

title: Projections & planets
text:
Planets
Module: planet (src/planet.f90). A planet_class carries the semi-major axis a, eccentricity e, flattening f, mean radius R, and an is_sphere flag. Two are predefined:








name
Shape
Parameters




"WGS84"
ellipsoid
a = 6 378 137 m, f = 1/298.257223563, e = 0.0818…, R ≈ 6.37122×10⁶ m


"Spherical Earth"
sphere
R ≈ 6.37122×10⁶ m, f ≈ 0, e = 0



WGS84 is the default. Geodesic distances and polygon areas on the ellipsoid are computed with Karney’s GeographicLib (geodesic.f90); the spherical planet uses great-circle formulas.

Distance & area routines



Routine
Purpose




planet_distance(a, f, lon1, lat1, lon2, lat2)
geodesic distance


spherical_distance(...)
great-circle distance


cartesian_distance(x1, y1, x2, y2)
Euclidean distance


planet_area(a, f, lons, lats)
geodesic polygon area


cartesian_area(x, y)
planar polygon area (shoelace)









TipExtensibility



Adding a planet means extending planet_init; adding a projection means adding forward/inverse routines and a method branch in projection_init / oblimap_projection. Broadening projection support (e.g. Lambert conformal, Mercator, rotated pole) is noted as a generalisation opportunity in the improvement proposal.
